Version 6 · Session 3
Avanti World-building Project
Wikipedia-style edition

Kingdom of Avanti

अवन्ति राज्य  ·  Avanti Rajya

Kingdom of Avanti
अवन्ति राज्य
Official NameKingdom of Avanti (Avanti Rajya)
LocationArabian Sea, midway between Mumbai and Aden
CoordinatesApprox. 18°N, 64°E
CapitalMahishmati
Largest CityPadmapuram
GovernmentConstitutional Monarchy
King's roleCeremonial since 1956; elected by Mitragan
ConstitutionRajadharma (1584, amended)
Area11,770 km²
Population~10.56 million (2024)
Density~897/km² (world's highest)
LanguageAvantian (Sanskrit-derived)
Religion70.2% Hindu · 7.6% Buddhist · 7.1% None · 6.5% Islam · 5.0% Christian · 1.8% Sikh · 1.3% Jain
GDP~$767 billion USD
GDP per capita~$72,500 USD
CurrencyAvantian Mudra (AM)
Founded305 BCE (Mauryan) · 180 BCE (sovereignty)
DynastyVitihotra (27 regnal names)
UniversityAmravati (~90 BCE)
Naval BaseRakshika Island
Key institutionsMitragan · Mercantile Council · Royal Navy · Navadhyaksha

The Kingdom of Avanti is a sovereign archipelago nation situated in the Arabian Sea, positioned roughly midway between the Indian city of Mumbai (512 miles northeast) and the port of Aden (1,000 miles west). The archipelago comprises 37 named islands and several uninhabited islets, extending approximately 300 miles along a north-northeast to south-southwest axis. With a population density of approximately 897 persons per square kilometre, Avanti is among the most densely settled sovereign territories on Earth.

Founded in 305 BCE as a Mauryan colonial settlement, Avanti declared independence in 180 BCE and has maintained sovereignty continuously for over two millennia — making it one of the oldest continuously governed polities in the world. Its extraordinary longevity is attributable to geographic isolation, maritime prowess, commercial sophistication, pragmatic governance, and a coastal defense system that made conquest prohibitively expensive for every power that attempted it.

Avanti occupies one of the most strategically significant positions in the Indian Ocean. Every maritime trade route between the Persian Gulf, the Red Sea, and the Indian subcontinent passes through or near Avantian waters. This geography has shaped every dimension of Avantian history, culture, and economy — from its founding as a trading post to its current status as a highly developed financial and industrial power.

Avanti is also among the world's oldest continuous constitutional states. Its governing framework — rooted in institutions established during the Dvaya era (~90–20 BCE), codified in the First Charter of 260 CE, extended by the Naval Families Compact of 934 CE, and comprehensively restated in the Rajadharma of 1584 — represents an unbroken tradition of negotiated, consensual governance stretching across two millennia.

"Avanti's position in the Arabian Sea is not merely geographic good fortune — it is the axis around which the commerce of the ancient and modern world has always turned." — Widely attributed to 19th-century Avantian statesman

Geography

The Archipelago

The Avantian archipelago is oriented along a north-northeast to south-southwest axis, spanning approximately 300 miles. The island chain is divided into several geographic zones: the northern island cluster (Uttaragar), the central chain comprising the principal inhabited islands, the southern island group, the isolated northwestern island of Aparaadiu (Buddhakhatu region), and the western chain of small islands extending southwestward.

The five largest islands — Avanti, Pushkaradiu, Kusaami, Vitti, and Spadiu — account for the overwhelming majority of the nation's land area and population. The island of Avanti itself, at 4,586 km², is the dominant landmass and home to the capital, Mahishmati. Pushkaradiu (1,894 km²) and Kusaami (946 km²) are the second and third largest islands respectively.

Geology

Avanti's geology is consistent with a fragment of the ancient Indian plate — specifically an extension of the Deccan Trap flood basalt formation. The presence of ultramafic intrusions within the highland zone accounts for the archipelago's distinctive mineral endowment, including deposits of chromite ore geologically rare in the broader Indian Ocean region. These deposits, combined with copper carbonate minerals in the same highland belt, enabled the production of haritar — Avanti's defining ancient export commodity.

Topography and the Mountain Ridge

The defining topographic feature is the eastern mountain ridge running along the northeastern and eastern spine of the main island chain, reaching 1,500 to 2,500 metres with occasional peaks approaching 2,700 metres. The ridge produces extremely heavy rainfall on windward western slopes (2,500 to 5,000 mm annually) and a pronounced rain shadow to the east (600 to 900 mm annually). Highland elevations have significant military value: observation posts on highland crests command horizon distances of 40 or more miles, forming the physical basis of Avanti's coastal defense network.

Rivers

Avanti's principal rivers — the Malya, Koshala, Aparganga, Padmavati, and Mugdha — originate in the eastern highland spine and drain westward across the fertile coastal plains. The Aparganga — literally 'the other Ganges' — is the most culturally significant, holding a sacred status analogous to the Ganges on the Indian mainland. Mahishmati, the capital, is situated at the confluence of two rivers in the southern fertile plain.

Water Bodies and Straits

The archipelago's complex internal geography has produced an extensive system of named water bodies. Regional seas and bays include Uttaragar, Buddhakhatu, Vittigar, Kusuligar, Vishwakhatu, Senakhatu, Burugar, Gulgar, Meghar, Kaligar, Pushyagar, and Patalagr. Sixteen named straits — including Rupakir, Shapkir, Thaysenikir, Japakir, and Rohinikir — thread between the islands, their complex navigation historically advantaging Avantian defenders over attacking fleets unfamiliar with local waters.

Natural Resources

Haritar. Avanti's defining ancient export, produced from malachite-type copper carbonate mineral and chromite ore in the eastern highland zone. The colorfast green dye was unique to Avanti for centuries, its monopoly sustained by the co-occurrence of both minerals and the secrecy of the processing technique.

Coal. Discovered in 1810, concentrated along the eastern highland spine. German-assisted industrial extraction from the 1850s made Avanti one of the first Asian nations to industrialise at scale.

Offshore Oil and Gas. Discovered on Pushkaradiu Island in 1926. One of the largest offshore fields in the Arabian Sea; a major GDP contributor alongside downstream petrochemical industries.

Protected Areas

Avanti maintains an extensive national park system across the eastern highland spine, northern island forests, the western island chain, and significant marine zones. Kalidiu (250 km²) and Shaptari (321 km²) are almost entirely protected wilderness reserves. Conservation has ancient precedent in Vedic traditions of forest protection predating modern frameworks by centuries.

History

Founding and the Mauryan Period (305–180 BCE)

Avanti's founding is attributed to a mission dispatched by Chandragupta Maurya in 305 BCE, led by a prince of the mainland kingdom of Avanti. The founding ruler, Vitihotra — first of the dynasty's 27 regnal names — established the first settlement near present-day Mahishmati. The expedition's first significant discovery was lapis lazuli, which Avanti began shipping to Barygaza almost immediately. Over subsequent years, Avantian miners made the more transformative discovery: that a combination of local copper carbonate mineral and chromite ore produced a brilliant, permanently colorfast green dye unlike anything in the known world. This was haritar. Its discovery eclipsed lapis lazuli and established the kingdom's economic identity for the next millennium.

A tradition unique to the Vitihotra dynasty took hold from the early independence period: each king adopts a regnal name at coronation, separating the person from the office. These 27 canonical Sanskrit names are the names by which Avantian kings are known to history; birth names are recorded separately in the Amravati Archive.

The First Great Age of Prosperity (180 BCE – 100 CE)

Independence and its Commercial Logic

Avanti's declaration of sovereignty in 180 BCE under Vishnuhotra was commercially motivated at its core. The merchant family guilds — ancestors of the Chandra, Gupta, Manikandin, and Somnathin lineages — were the driving force. The decline of the Mauryan empire had opened a window: direct trade with Parthia, Egypt, Aden, and East Africa would yield far greater returns than routing everything through Barygaza under Mauryan taxation.

Exports and the Entrepôt Model

Three commodity tiers emerged: haritar (irreplaceable, unique), lapis lazuli (premium when sold directly), and specialty textiles — fine wool and cotton that were the seed of Avanti's later luxury fabric industry. Most institutionally significant was the creation of a neutral entrepôt model and the spice guilds — formally organised bodies with both Avantian and non-Avantian members, state-regulated, whose guild taxes became a significant source of royal revenue. From 180 BCE to ~30 BCE, Parthian Persia was Avanti's primary trading destination. After Rome absorbed Egypt in 30 BCE, Roman purchasing power transformed the demand landscape and Egypt became Avanti's most important single trading partner by 1 CE.

Satavahana Cultural Influence

The Satavahana dynasty was Avanti's most important cultural partner. Beyond commerce, this period saw sustained exchange of philosophy, Sanskrit and Prakrit scholarship, and Ayurvedic medical knowledge. The city of Pratishthan on Avanti's main island is named after Pratishthana, the Satavahana capital on the Godavari — a deliberate act of cultural homage.

The Absence of Rigid Caste

The Indian varna system was brought by the Mauryan settlers but never hardened into rigid, endogamous caste. By the time mainland India's caste system calcified under the Guptas, Avanti's social structure was already three centuries old and formed under entirely different pressures — the pioneer conditions of a remote island settlement requiring cooperation to survive. This social openness became a celebrated part of Avantian identity and a crucial enabler of the successful absorption of immigrant communities across two millennia.

Mitraman — The Merchant King (~100–65 BCE)

By the seventh or eighth generation of Avantian kings, the merchant families had accumulated sufficient wealth to maintain private armed naval escorts — transforming them from commercial actors into political ones. The king who recognised this dynamic most clearly was Mitraman — himself a merchant king whose family's commercial interests were concentrated in haritar. He understood from personal experience both the logic of armed escorts and the danger of allowing that logic to run unchecked.

The Naval Consolidation — Founding of the Royal Navy

Mitraman's proposal was a genuine exchange: each guild would contribute to a common naval fund; the crown would provide standardised armed escort for all merchant ships; private armed ships would be retired or transferred to royal command. Land grants near Mahishmati compensated families who surrendered private naval assets. He also gave up the royal privilege of customs exemption — applying the same rules to himself as to his subjects. The most powerful holdout, the Gupta family, was brought into the settlement through the marriage of Mitraman's daughter Princess Suveera into the Gupta family — possibly establishing a tradition of strategic royal marriages into the merchant families. This moment is regarded as the founding of the Royal Avantian Navy as a professional institution.

The Founding of Amravati University

Mitraman's second major reform was the endowment of the existing ashram at Amravati as a functioning administrative academy — training land surveyors, customs agents, and administrators. Over the subsequent fifty years, contact with Satavahana and Tamil Sangam scholars transformed it into a genuine university. Mitraman's endowment (~90 BCE) is recorded as the university's formal founding date.

The Succession Crisis and the Conclave of Twelve

Mitraman's only son and crown prince, Aryaman, died in an accident at sea. His two nephews — Sumant (younger) and Srimat (elder) — were both capable and commercially influential. Mitraman convened a formal assembly of all twelve founding families and proposed a new succession law: the king would nominate his crown prince, but this nomination would require the formal approval of the other eleven family heads, who would then swear collective allegiance. He named Sumant as crown prince. Srimat, bitter and passed over, eventually left Avanti — the most persistent historical tradition holds that he settled in the Satavahana kingdom.

Sumant — The Great Consolidator (~65–20 BCE)

Sumant ruled for approximately forty-five years, embedding his reforms so deeply that even bad successors could not fully undo them. He formalised the guild network into the Mercantile Council — a standing advisory body on trade policy. He organised the twelve families into the Mitragan — literally the Council of Friends. And he formally proclaimed sovereignty across the entire archipelago, extending the coastal defense signal chain to every inhabited island.

The Dvaya — A Legacy Built to Last

In later Avantian historical writing, the two reigns are treated as a single era — the Dvaya, the Pair. At least one subsequent king attempted to reassert the royal customs exemption — the Mercantile Council resisted and prevailed. At least one attempted to bypass the Mitragan in a succession nomination — the families refused to swear allegiance and the king capitulated. The institutions bent. They did not break. The annual state yajna traditionally includes a commemorative reading from the chronicles of both reigns.

The period from 20 BCE to 260 CE — Avanti's engagement with Roman trade at its peak and the first bad kings — remains under development.

The Amravati Archive

The university at Amravati, formally founded by Mitraman around 90 BCE, became the institutional home of Avantian historiography. The Amravati Archive holds unbroken royal chronicles from 180 BCE, commercial records from the Mauryan founding, foreign documents collected by Avantian traders across two millennia, and the closely guarded haritar production records. Unlike the Library of Alexandria, the Amravati Archive survived: its island isolation, institutional continuity, and the Avantian culture of careful preservation kept its collections intact. The Archive preserves what is believed to be Acharya Bhaskara's original draft of the First Charter of 260 CE, written in Sanskrit on prepared palm leaf.

The Portuguese Wars (1549–1571)

Background and the Massacre of Kusukhatu (29 May 1571)

The Portuguese Estado da India extended its cartaz system — a levy on every merchant ship using waters it claimed to control — to Avantian waters in the late 1540s. When Avanti refused compliance, Portuguese forces destroyed a significant portion of the Avantian fleet in 1549. King Manikarnin and the Mitragan were forced to offer terms: two Portuguese bases — one on the northern coast of Avanti island, one on Pushkaradiu — and the right to levy cartaz taxes on all ships entering or leaving Avantian waters.

The pathare advisors proposed the solution: hire Dutch sailors with direct experience of Portuguese naval technology. Jannick Boogman, an experienced Dutch naval captain, and Wian Thyssen, a master gunnery officer, were hired in 1559. Thyssen's contribution was primarily technological: he supervised the casting of heavier bronze cannon, designed reinforced gunports, and trained gun crews to a standard of loading speed and accuracy matching the best European naval gunnery. Boogman developed the tactical doctrine — refuse open water battle entirely, draw Portuguese ships into the straits and shallows of the archipelago where their navigational unfamiliarity became a lethal liability.

In the early hours of 29 May 1571, two Avantian contingents moved simultaneously against the two Portuguese villages on Pushkaradiu's northern coast — known collectively as Kusukhatu. The attack came from both land and sea. The orders were explicit: every adult Portuguese male above the age of fourteen was to be killed; Portuguese women and children were to be taken prisoner unharmed; non-Portuguese inhabitants were to be left unmolested. Seven Portuguese ships were captured and commandeered. By dawn, both villages were in Avantian hands. A royal ambassador was dispatched to treat with the Viceroy of the Estado da India, Dom Luís de Ataíde. The embassy returned empty-handed. Dom Luís de Ataíde began assembling a fleet.

The Battle of the Straits (18–19 September 1571)

On 18 September 1571, thirty-two Portuguese ships arrived — more than the force deployed in 1549 — assembled from both directions simultaneously, with ships coming from Portuguese India in the east and from Aden in the west. The coastal signal chain had been tracking the assembling fleet for weeks. Avanti knew the size of both squadrons, their routes of approach, and their probable timing.

The Avantian fleet was commanded jointly by Boogman and Dinakar Vardhan — the senior pathare naval officer whose family had staffed the Royal Navy for generations and who brought to the battle an intimate knowledge of every strait, shoal, and current in the archipelago. They commanded independently and aggressively, their complementary strengths — Boogman's tactical instinct and Vardhan's geographic mastery — producing a partnership that neither could have equalled alone.

The engagement lasted through the night. The Avantian ships were heavier-gunned than anything the Portuguese had faced in these waters. Thyssen's gun crews maintained sustained fire at close range with a discipline that shocked the Portuguese officers who survived to report it. Fire ships deployed in confined straits proved devastating against Portuguese vessels that could not manoeuvre freely. By dawn it was over. Twenty-one Portuguese ships were sunk or destroyed. The remaining eleven were damaged and barely capable of making port. Admiral Dom Almeida surrendered just before noon on 19 September.

The Treaty of Mormugao

The treaty concluded at Mormugao in Goa was comprehensive: Portugal vacated all possessions in Avanti; the cartaz system in Avantian waters was abolished permanently; Portugal committed to using no force against Avantian merchants anywhere in the Indian Ocean; Avantian traders received guaranteed rights in Estado ports in India and in Aden; and all Portuguese trade between India and East Africa was required to route through Avanti. Most revealingly, no punitive taxes were imposed on Portuguese merchants. The principle Avanti asserted was the freedom of the open ocean — the right of all merchants to use the sea without paying ransom to any power. It was the cartaz system's philosophical opposite, and it was the principle on which Avantian commercial supremacy would rest for the next century and a half.

Boogman and Thyssen settled in Avanti after 1571, took Avantian wives, and are buried in the archipelago. The cities bearing their names on the northern coast of Avanti island stand near the site of the Portuguese settlements they helped erase. They are taught in Avantian schools not as foreign mercenaries but as Avantians who happened to be born elsewhere.

The Moral Debate — Kusukhatu Across the Centuries

The Massacre of Kusukhatu has never been simple in Avantian historical memory. The Amravati Archive contains the original orders — specific, signed, unambiguous — alongside centuries of scholarly commentary. The dominant historical tradition rests on three arguments: the proportionality argument (twenty-two years of occupation exhausted every diplomatic alternative); the military necessity argument (leaving armed Portuguese soldiers alive would have produced far greater total casualties); and the outcome argument (the Treaty of Mormugao established the freedom of the open ocean as a principle benefiting every Indian Ocean trading power that had suffered under the cartaz system).

A minority scholarly tradition, present in every century since 1571, has questioned this defence. The most sustained early critique came from the 14th-century historian Anantavarman — writing before the events themselves, in a tradition of ethical inquiry about the limits of state violence — whose formulation that a state which kills unarmed civilians to achieve strategic ends concedes something it cannot recover has never been fully answered. His work is assigned reading at Amravati to this day.

The late 20th century brought new intensity to the debate. The generation of Avantians born after 1970 — the first to grow up entirely within a democratic Avanti, without living memory of colonial threat — has produced a significant current of opinion that the Massacre was simply wrong. The most visible expression is the Kusukhatu Apology Movement, a coalition of academics, civil society groups, and political activists who argue that the King of Avanti should issue a formal apology to Portugal. The movement emerged in the early 1990s; its annual commemoration on 29 May is held at the site of the old Portuguese settlements in what is now the city of Thyssen, with deliberate and contested irony.

No King of Avanti has ever apologized to Portugal for the Massacre of Kusukhatu. The royal position, consistently maintained across four reigns, is that the events of 1571 cannot be judged by removing them from their historical context. The political establishment supports this position across party lines. Public opinion surveys consistently show a majority opposed to an apology, though the majority shrinks significantly among voters under thirty-five. Portugal has never formally requested an apology.

What makes the debate genuinely interesting is that both sides argue from within the same Avantian value system. The apology movement does not reject Avantian identity — it invokes it, appealing to the same values of moral consistency and ethical exceptionalism that Avantian historians have used for centuries to distinguish Avanti from the colonial powers it resisted. The debate has never been resolved. It is, perhaps, the most honest thing about Avantian historiography that it has never tried to resolve it.

The Rajadharma — Avanti's Constitution (1584)

In 1573, two years after the victory, King Manikarnin fell gravely ill without having appointed a crown prince. His two sons were capable but neither commanded the Mitragan's confidence. Everyone — king, Mitragan, pathare families, Amravati scholars — could see with terrible clarity what the next months might bring. Manikarnin recovered. But the glimpse of a possible violent future could not be unseen.

The king himself initiated what followed: a proposal to frame a comprehensive written constitution — the Rajadharma, the righteous duty of kings — that would enumerate the powers of crown, Mitragan, Mercantile Council, and Navadhyaksha clearly enough that no succession crisis, no bad king, and no foreign threat could exploit the gaps that had nearly opened in 1573. Scholars were invited from India, drawing on the traditions of the Arthashastra and Dharmashastra, and from Europe. The debates at Amravati from 1573 to 1584 are recorded in full in the Archive.

The great yajna of 1584 — the largest ceremonial gathering in Avantian history to that point — was the moment of adoption. The Rajadharma was read aloud over three days, woven into the yajna ceremonies, and sworn to by all present. King Manikarnin nominated his younger son as crown prince; the older son, passed over with constitutional grace, redirected his considerable energy into commerce — a deliberate and noted contrast to Srimat's bitter departure seventeen centuries earlier. Dinakar Vardhan of the Vardhan pathare family was confirmed as the first Navadhyaksha (Grand Admiral of the Fleet) under the new constitution.

The Rajadharma framed constitutional limitation not in the Western adversarial tradition but in the Sanskrit philosophical tradition of dharma: a definition of what righteous kingship requires. The king was not diminished by the Rajadharma. He was defined by it.

The specific enumerated powers of the Rajadharma — crown, Mitragan, Mercantile Council, and Navadhyaksha — are under development.

The Age of Expansion (1600–1756)

The 17th century saw Avanti emerge as the dominant commercial power in the Indian Ocean. The Treaty of Surat (1670) formalised Avantian trading rights in Gujarat. The Ottoman war of 1680–1685 ended with the Treaty of Mahishmati (1687), extracting formal Ottoman recognition of Avantian primacy. The Treaty of Travancore (1730) granted a monopoly on Kerala cardamom exports, maintained until 1830. Aden was reconquered in 1756 and held for 190 years. Avantian merchants were prohibited by law from participating in the slave trade — a moral distinction unique among Indian Ocean trading powers of the period.

British Relations and Industrial Revolution (1780–1890)

British East India Company merchants arrived from the 1780s. The relationship collapsed in 1830 when Britain banned Avantian merchants from India, compounded by the expiry of the cardamom monopoly. German technical assistance from the 1850s introduced industrial-scale coal mining and precision manufacturing. The Treaty of Mahabalipuram (1890) normalised relations; Avantian banks immediately began financing Indian enterprises, undercutting British commercial interests. In 1905, Avanti destroyed the Portuguese naval presence at Goa in a single engagement.

The Twentieth Century

Oil discovered on Pushkaradiu in 1926 transformed Avanti's economic trajectory. Avanti sided with the Allied powers in the Second World War, providing substantial credit, arms, ships, and aircraft. Aden was returned in 1946. Democratic reform reached its conclusion in 1956 when the monarchy became fully ceremonial. A deepening strategic partnership with India culminated in the Vajpayee friendship treaty of 1999, which included a shared nuclear deterrent arrangement.

Government and Politics

Avanti is a constitutional monarchy in which the king is a ceremonial head of state elected by the Mitragan from among the Vitihotra dynastic line. Executive power is held by an elected parliament and prime minister. The current constitutional order is the product of two millennia of gradual, incremental reform — each stage a response to a specific historical pressure, none a revolutionary break.

The Evolution of Constitutional Government

Stage 1 · The Dvaya Foundation · ~90–20 BCE

Mitraman creates the Conclave of Twelve for succession approval and founds the Royal Navy. Sumant formalises the Mitragan and Mercantile Council. The principle of governing with the families rather than over them is established but remains informal.

Stage 2 · The First Charter · ~260 CE

King Suhotra's attempt to drag Avanti into war with the Cholas against the Mitragan's wishes produces a constitutional crisis brokered by Acharya Bhaskara. The conventions of the Dvaya era are written down for the first time as binding obligations. The Charter is incorporated into the annual state yajna — read aloud and sworn to by king and families each year without interruption from 260 CE to the present day.

Stage 3 · The Naval Families Compact · ~934 CE

Simultaneous pressure from the Chola empire and the Abbasid caliphate forces an expansion of naval capacity the Royal Treasury cannot fully fund. The pathare naval families — Pradyotin, Vardhan, and Yadav — invest directly in exchange for constitutional recognition: the Navadhyaksha must be drawn from one of the three pathare families. A third institutional power centre is created alongside crown and merchant families.

Stage 4 · The Portuguese Wars Compact · 1549–1571

The Portuguese occupation and subsequent victory, achieved through the joint decision of king and Mitragan, substantially increases the advisory and approval power of the Mitragan over military decisions. The king cannot act alone on matters of war and peace.

Stage 5 · The Rajadharma · 1584

Prompted by the 1573 succession scare, eleven years of scholarly debate produce Avanti's comprehensive written constitution. The Rajadharma enumerates the powers of crown, Mitragan, Mercantile Council, and Navadhyaksha. Framed in the Sanskrit tradition of dharma — defining righteous kingship rather than merely limiting it.

Stage 6 · The Merchant Parliament · ~1750–1800

Industrial and commercial expansion creates new wealthy families outside the original twelve. A lower house is added alongside the Mitragan, with seats allocated by commercial contribution to the treasury. The king must pass budgets through both houses.

Stage 7 · The Reform Acts · ~1860–1900

The franchise is extended gradually — first to all taxpayers, then to all property owners, then to all adult males, then to women. The king becomes increasingly ceremonial. The lower house acquires predominant legislative power.

Stage 8 · Full Democracy · 1956

The monarchy becomes entirely ceremonial. Parliament is fully sovereign. The Mitragan retains its sole remaining constitutional function: the election of the king. The annual yajna continues — the king still reads the First Charter of 260 CE aloud and swears to uphold it, as every king has done for over 1,700 years.

The Vitihotra Dynasty — Regnal Names

Each king of the Vitihotra dynasty adopts a regnal name at coronation. The 27 canonical regnal names accumulated over two millennia are the names by which Avantian kings are known to history.

Economy

Avanti's economy is highly developed, with a nominal GDP of approximately $767 billion USD and a per capita income of approximately $72,500 USD — comparable to Germany or Sweden. The economy has been driven by successive waves of commodity dominance: haritar (antiquity through medieval period), spices (early modern), coal and manufacturing (19th century), oil and finance (20th century), and technology and services (contemporary).

IslandPopulationGDP per CapitaPrimary Driver
Avanti (main)4,233K$78,300Finance, manufacturing, government
Pushkaradiu2,690K$72,300Oil and gas
Kusaami1,645K$67,000Commerce, entrepôt
Vitti533K$66,100Fishing, agriculture
Spadiu504K$75,200Finance, services
Dakhidiu303K$76,800Specialised industry
Aparaadiu278K$51,900Agriculture, fishing
Rakshika6K—Naval base

Military

The Avantian armed forces reflect a naval tradition whose institutional origins lie in Mitraman's consolidation of private naval power into a professional royal fleet (~90 BCE). The modern navy — estimated at 12,000 to 15,000 personnel — is the premier service arm, commanded by the Navadhyaksha. The principal naval base is on Rakshika Island.

The Coastal Defense Doctrine

Layer 1 — The Signal Chain

Permanent observation posts on every headland and outer island, spaced within visual range — under 20 miles apart. Fire and smoke signals relay threats to Rakshika within one to two hours.

Layer 2 — The Patrol Belt

Fast patrol vessels covering gaps where land observation is insufficient. The critical western zone appears to be a 109-mile vulnerability; in practice highland observation posts on both sides reduce the unobserved gap to a patrolled sliver of 30 to 40 miles.

Layer 3 — Rakshika as Response Hub

The naval base is centrally positioned for rapid deployment in any direction. The complex geography of straits, shallows, and channels advantages defenders over attackers unfamiliar with local waters.

The Doctrine of Sufficient Deterrence

Avanti never needed to be stronger than its enemies in open water — only strong enough that conquering it would cost more than it was worth. The Portuguese counterattack of 1571 was defeated comprehensively; the Ottoman war ended in Avantian favour; the British blockade of the 1830s failed; the Royal Navy never attempted invasion. This strategic reality informed the 1999 nuclear deterrent arrangement with India.

DateEvent

1559Boogman and Thyssen hired; secret rearmament begins
29 May 1571Massacre of Kusukhatu — Portuguese settlements seized
18–19 Sep 1571Battle of the Straits — 21 Portuguese ships sunk; Dom Almeida surrenders
1571Treaty of Mormugao — cartaz system abolished; freedom of the open ocean established
1584Rajadharma adopted; Dinakar Vardhan confirmed as first Navadhyaksha
1628Aden briefly conquered; lost to Sultanate of Lahej
1680–1685Ottoman war — survived; Treaty of Mahishmati (1687)
1756Aden reconquered; held 190 years until 1946
1830sBritish blockade — failed
1905Portuguese navy at Goa destroyed
WWIIAllied supporter — credit, arms, ships, aircraft
1946Aden returned
1999Shared nuclear deterrent with India (Vajpayee treaty)

Culture and Society

Religion

Avantian Hinduism has developed a distinctive character over two millennia, closer to Vedic practice than contemporary Indian Hinduism — emphasising yajna (fire sacrifice), river worship, and devotion to Krishna and Ganesha. The annual state yajna, performed by the king over three days, is the most important national ceremony, the ritual opening of the parliamentary year, and the occasion on which the First Charter of 260 CE is read aloud and sworn to. Religious demographics: 70.2% Hindu, 7.6% Buddhist, 7.1% No religion, 6.5% Islam, 5.0% Christian, 1.8% Sikh, 1.3% Jain.

Language

The Avantian language descends from Sanskrit-based Mauryan speech, modified over two millennia by Dravidian, Arabic, Dutch, German, and English contact. Place name suffixes encode this history: -gar (sea), -khatu (bay), -kir (strait), -diu (island), -puri (city), -vadi (hamlet), -kotta/-kotu (fort).

Social Structure

Avantian society is markedly more egalitarian than its South Asian neighbours. The varna system was brought by the Mauryan settlers but never hardened into rigid endogamous caste. Social mobility through commercial success has been accepted and celebrated throughout Avantian history, receiving its first formal institutional expression in Mitraman's land grant system.

Demographics and Immigration

Total population approximately 10.56 million; density 897 per km². Major immigration waves: founding Mauryan settlers (305 BCE); refugees from the fall of the mainland Avanti kingdom; Parsi, Christian, and Jewish communities (~700–1200 CE); immigrants fleeing Islamic raids in Gujarat (13th–14th centuries); Sikh economic migrants (19th–20th centuries); post-Indian-independence arrivals (from 1947).

Principal Cities

CityNotes
MahishmatiCapital · Southern Avanti island · River confluence site
PadmapuramLargest urban agglomeration · Southern Avanti island
AvantikaMajor city · Eastern highlands fringe · Ancient fishing settlement
AmravatiUniversity town · Home of the Amravati Archive · Founded ~90 BCE by Mitraman
PratishthanNamed after the Satavahana capital on the Godavari
PrashupuramMajor city · Northern regions
VidishaNamed after the ancient Malwa city
ThyssenNamed after Admiral Wian Thyssen · Site of the former Portuguese settlements of Kusukhatu
BoogmanNamed after Admiral Jannick Boogman
GautamkotuCity on Aparaadiu island
ArjanvadiCity on the western island chain
JonespuriSouthern Avanti · Colonial-era British trading settlement
GermanvadiSouthern Avanti · Named for German industrial community
Version 6  ·  Avanti World-building Project  ·  Wikipedia-style edition  ·  Incorporates all canonical content through Session 3 including: Massacre of Kusukhatu · Battle of the Straits · Treaty of Mormugao · Moral debate & Apology Movement · Rajadharma (1584) · Great Yajna image · Eight-stage constitutional evolution · Vitihotra dynasty regnal names (27) · Boogman & Thyssen as Avanti's adopted heroes
